name: Gnostic Cosmology
shortDescription: Gnostic cosmology describes a universe ruled by a flawed creator (the Demiurge) separate from the true, higher God. Salvation is achieved through secret knowledge (gnosis) that allows the soul to escape the material realm and return to the divine.
description: Gnostic cosmology presents a complex and varied worldview that departs significantly from mainstream Christian theology. At its core is the belief that the material universe is inherently flawed and imperfect, not created by the true, benevolent God, but by a lesser, often ignorant or malevolent being known as the Demiurge. This Demiurge, often identified with the Old Testament God, is seen as a flawed creator who traps souls within the physical world. The true God, a transcendent and unknowable Monad, exists beyond the Demiurge's realm. The material world is therefore considered a prison, and human beings are seen as possessing a divine spark trapped within a corruptible body. Salvation, in Gnostic thought, is not achieved through faith or good works, but through gnosis, a secret and intuitive knowledge of the true nature of reality. This knowledge allows the individual soul to recognize its divine origin and to escape the cycle of reincarnation and the Demiurge's control, ultimately returning to the Monad. Different Gnostic sects held varying views on the nature of the Demiurge, the role of Christ, and the path to salvation. Some viewed Christ as an emissary of the true God, sent to awaken humanity to their divine potential, while others interpreted the biblical narrative through a Gnostic lens, often reversing traditional roles and interpretations. Common themes include the inherent duality of existence (spirit vs. matter, good vs. evil), the importance of esoteric knowledge, and the rejection of the material world as the ultimate reality. Figures like Yaldabaoth and Sabaoth are often prominent within Gnostic creation myths, representing aspects of the Demiurge or his offspring. The Holy Spirit is sometimes reinterpreted as a feminine divine principle, Sophia, who attempts to rectify the errors of the Demiurge.

sources.title: Gnosis: The Nature and History of Gnosticism
sources.author: Kurt Rudolph
